name: Sierra Salvada y Pico del Fraile — circular by the ZEC Monte Santiago
name_original: Sierra Salvada y Pico del Fraile — circular por el ZEC Monte Santiago
region_intro: Jogging around Monumento natural del Monte Santiago offers routes through a landscape characterized by extensive beech forests and dramatic limestone ridges of the Sierra Salvada. The region features a diverse ecosystem, providing shaded paths and visually engaging backdrops for running. Key natural features include the Salto del Nervión viewpoint, offering views of Spain's highest waterfall, and unique karstic terrain with impressive cliffs. This area provides a stimulating environment for runners seeking varied outdoor experiences. Best jogging routes around Monumento natural del Monte Santiago The most popular jogging route is Sierra Salvada y Pico del Fraile — circular by the ZEC Monte Santiago, a 5.1 miles (8.2 km) trail that takes 56 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the Sierra Salvada. Another top favourite among local runners is Esquina de Rubén Viewpoint – Salto del Nervión Viewpoint loop from Monumento Natural del Monte Santiago, a moderate 5.8 miles (9.4 km) path. This route leads to the Salto del Nervión viewpoint, showcasing the region's dramatic cliffs. Local runners also love the Running loop from Aparcamiento de Hoyalante, a 6.1 miles (9.9 km) trail leading through forested areas, often completed in about 1 hour 5 minutes. Jogging around Monumento natural del Monte Santiago is defined by dense beech forests, limestone cliffs, and viewpoints overlooking the Salto del Nervión. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy forest paths to more challenging trails with greater elevation gain. The routes in Monumento natural del Monte Santiago are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from 3 reviews. More than 300 runners have used komoot to explore Monumento natural del Monte Santiago's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Monumento natural del Monte Santiago?
A: There are &lt;strong&gt;43 dedicated running routes&lt;/strong&gt; in Monumento natural del Monte Santiago. These trails offer a variety of experiences, from easy forest paths to more challenging ascents, catering to different fitness levels.

Q: Are there any easy jogging routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Monumento natural del Monte Santiago offers several easy running routes. The area is known for its well-marked trails, with &lt;strong&gt;3 routes specifically categorized as easy&lt;/strong&gt;, making them suitable for beginners or families looking for a gentle jog through the natural landscape.

Q: Can I find routes that lead to the Salto del Nervión waterfall?
A: Absolutely. The region is famous for the Salto del Nervión, Spain's highest waterfall. You can find routes that offer stunning views of this natural wonder. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20160632" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Esquina de Rubén Viewpoint – Salto del Nervión Viewpoint loop from Monumento Natural del Monte Santiago&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 5.8-mile path that leads directly to the viewpoint, showcasing the dramatic cliffs.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running trails?
A: The running trails in Monumento natural del Monte Santiago feature diverse terrain. You'll encounter extensive, lush &lt;strong&gt;beech forests&lt;/strong&gt;, providing shaded paths, as well as areas with pine and Holm oak. The landscape also includes dramatic &lt;strong&gt;limestone ridges of the Sierra Salvada&lt;/strong&gt;, with karstic formations like cliffs, caves, and dolines, offering a visually engaging backdrop for your run.

Q: Are there any circular jogging routes in the area?
A: Yes, many of the running paths in Monumento natural del Monte Santiago are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1051319804/sierra-salvada-y-pico-del-fraile-circular-by-the-zec-monte-santiago" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Salvada y Pico del Fraile — circular by the ZEC Monte Santiago&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 5.1-mile trail offering views of the Sierra Salvada.

Q: What natural features or wildlife might I encounter while running?
A: Monumento natural del Monte Santiago is a rich ecosystem. You'll run through beautiful beech forests and witness the dramatic Sierra Salvada amphitheater. Keep an eye out for diverse wildlife; the area is home to griffon vultures, golden eagles, peregrine falcons, and Egyptian vultures soaring above the cliffs. You might also spot deer, wild boars, and squirrels within the forests.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks along the running paths?
A: Yes, the region has historical significance. You can find remnants of the past, such as a recreation of an old wolf trap (lobera), which offers a glimpse into 18th and 19th-century practices. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3057117" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruins of the Monastery of Santiago de Legrériz&lt;/a&gt; are another historical point of interest in the area.

Q: Where can I park when visiting the running routes?
A: Parking is generally available at key access points to the trails. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24569061" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Aparcamiento de Hoyalante&lt;/a&gt; starts from a parking area, indicating convenient access for runners.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Monumento natural del Monte Santiago?
A: The running routes in Monumento natural del Monte Santiago are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average score of &lt;strong&gt;4.7 stars from 3 reviews&lt;/strong&gt;. Runners often praise the dense beech forests, the dramatic limestone cliffs, and the stunning viewpoints overlooking the Salto del Nervión, highlighting the stimulating and beautiful environment for outdoor activities.

Q: Are there any challenging running routes for experienced runners?
A: Yes, for experienced runners seeking a greater challenge, Monumento natural del Monte Santiago offers more difficult routes. There are &lt;strong&gt;23 routes categorized as difficult&lt;/strong&gt;, featuring greater elevation gain and more demanding terrain. An example includes the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24568942" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iturrigorri / Tologorri – Iturrigorri loop from Gorobel mendilerroa BBE&lt;/a&gt;, which involves significant elevation changes.

Q: What are some notable viewpoints to include in a running tour?
A: Beyond the famous Salto del Nervión viewpoint, the area offers several other stunning vistas. You can enjoy views from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3968698" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Esquina de Rubén Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, or take in panoramic scenes from &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3079407" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of Pico del Fraile&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3079416" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Txarlazo&lt;/a&gt;, both prominent summits in the region.
tips: Set of cliffs that appear along the Sierra Salvada and that go in a northerly direction. On the south side, the Burgalesa, the loins develop in the direction of the Río Nabón.

This rocky peak is vertical on all its flanks, which makes it necessary to use leather straps during the ascent. It was first climbed in 1924 by Ángel Sopeña, precursor of climbing in the Basque Country.

Its name comes from its resemblance to the figure of an abbot.
